title: How to Price Screen Repairs for Maximum Profit
author: Mobibix
keywords: how much to charge mobile repair
excerpt: Are you undercharging for complex repairs and losing money? Or overcharging and driving customers to competitors? Here is how to scientifically price a screen repair.
content:
## Introduction

Pricing a screen repair is the most contentious decision a mobile shop owner makes. 

If a generic LCD panel costs ₹1,500 from your distributor, what do you charge the customer? ₹2,000? ₹2,500? ₹4,000? 

Many inexperienced owners simply multiply the part cost by a random percentage or blindly copy the price of the shop down the street. This "guesswork" strategy either leaves money on the table or destroys consumer trust.

Here is a scientific framework for establishing highly profitable, defendable screen repair prices.

---

## 1. The "Perceived Value" vs. Part Cost Disconnect

The fundamental rule of repair pricing: **Customers do not pay for parts; they pay for outcomes.**

If an OLED screen replacement for an old Samsung A-series costs you ₹3,000, and a replacement screen for a flagship iPhone 14 Pro Max also happens to cost you ₹3,000 (due to a rare supplier discount), you should *absolutely not* charge both customers the same final price.

The customer with the iPhone 14 Pro Max has a ₹1,20,000 device. The intrinsic value of restoring that device is astronomically higher than restoring a ₹15,000 Samsung. Your risk profile in handling the iPhone is also significantly higher. 

**Rule 1: Always factor the current market value of the overall device into the repair margin.**

---

## 2. Tiered Quality Options (Good, Better, Best)

Never offer just one price. If you only give customers a single option, the decision is binary: "Yes or No."

If you provide three tiers, the decision shifts to "Which one?"

**Example Pricing for an iPhone 13 Screen:**
1.  **"Good" (Aftermarket LCD):** ₹3,500. *(Lower brightness, thicker bezels. Best for customers on a tight budget who are giving the phone to their kids.)*
2.  **"Better" (Premium Aftermarket OLED):** ₹5,500. *(Near-original colors and touch response. The sweet spot.)*
3.  **"Best" (Original/Pulled OEM):** ₹9,000. *(Exact factory specifications. 1-year warranty.)*

Most customers (70%) will predictably choose the "Better" option. By contrasting it against the ₹9,000 OEM option, the ₹5,500 price feels incredibly reasonable.

---

## 3. Calculating the Fully Burdened Labor Rate

Your technician's time is not free. You cannot just add ₹500 "profit" to the part cost and call it a day. You must calculate your **Fully Burdened Hourly Rate**.

*   Technician Salary + Rent + Utilities / Expected Productive Repair Hours in a Month.
*   If those total expenses are ₹60,000/month, and the shop is open 200 hours, your shop costs ₹300 per hour just to exist.

If an intricate iPad glass separation takes 3 hours, your baseline cost to perform that repair *before the part* is ₹900. If you only charged a ₹1,000 markup on the part, you made exactly ₹100 in net profit. 

---

## 4. The "While-You-Wait" Premium

Time is money. If a customer requires an immediate, "jump-the-queue" repair within 30 minutes because they have a flight to catch, charge a premium.

Offer a standard 24-hour turnaround for the base price, and a ₹500 or ₹1,000 "Rush Diagnostic & Repair Fee."

Business professionals will happily pay a 20% premium simply to guarantee they aren't without their device overnight.

---

## Conclusion

Pricing is a blend of psychology, strict accounting, and hardware risk assessment. By implementing tiered options and calculating your true hourly liability, you create defendable prices that maximize margins without alienating budget-conscious clients.
